TextENGNew: The moment club activities wrapped up, Rena was gone—bolted straight home.
Apparently she'd spotted something irresistibly cute at her usual treasure-hunting spot.
changeReason: Old translation uses the pronoun 'he' when referring to Rena (a female character); new translation correctly uses 'her.'

TextENGNew: So for once it was just Mion and me making the walk home together.
She pouted the whole way, but she was dutifully carrying my bag all the same.
"That spot she's always talking about... it's a garbage heap on the other side of those woods over there."
She said it with a resigned shrug. Fair enough—it wasn't exactly the kind of place a girl her age should be getting starry-eyed over.
"But seriously, what kind of treasure are you going to find in an illegal dumping site? I just hope she doesn't hurt herself."
"Ahahahaha! Relax, she'll be fine—it's not like it's her first time. Besides, that used to be the dam construction site, so there's all sorts of stuff there."
"All... sorts of stuff...?"
"Yeah, a lot happened there. Like, battles! Sit-ins, protests, that kind of thing!"
changeReason: Old translation says 'this great battle' (singular/specific) and 'strikes,' but the Japanese 戦いとか means 'battles' (plural/general) and 座りこみ means 'sit-ins,' not 'strikes.'

TextENGNew: Calling it a battle sounded intense, but I decided to hear her out.
Mion launched into the story with wide, animated gestures.
"So they wanted to flood Hinamizawa—sink the whole village under a reservoir... That really is something else."
"The government people were absolutely relentless. So full of themselves! And once they realized money wasn't going to settle it, they started pulling every dirty trick in the book."
Mion spoke as if she'd witnessed the whole thing firsthand, her voice rising with indignation.
"I'm amazed you actually won. You were up against the national government, weren't you?"
changeReason: Old translation says 'federal government,' but Japan has a unitary national government, not a federal one; new translation correctly says 'national government.'

TextENGNew: "The village head and the influential families filed petitions everywhere. They went to Tokyo, worked all kinds of back channels with politicians... And eventually the whole plan got shelved. A total victory for us! Ahahahahaha!"
changeReason: Old says 'signed petitions' omitting that they petitioned widely (方々に), and 'struck deals with legislators' where Japanese 根回し means behind-the-scenes groundwork rather than deal-making.

TextENGNew: "There wasn't any... violence, was there? Like assault... or, you know, murder—"
"There wasn't."
She cut me off with a clean finality.
Something about it sat wrong with me.
There was a period at the end of those words.
Was she telling me not to dig any further? Or was it something else—
Maybe this wasn't the kind of thing you just casually pried into.
changeReason: Old implies Mion is hiding the truth, but the Japanese suggests Keiichi reflecting that this wasn't something to casually dig into--a shift from suspicion of Mion to self-awareness about prying.

TextENGNew: My restless curiosity drooped inside my chest, not knowing where to go.
"What's that look for? You look like you still want to be teased."
"N-No I don't!"
"Here you go. This old man has personally delivered Kei-chan's bag all the way to his house! See you tomorrow. Bye-bye!"
changeReason: Old translation omits Mion's characteristic self-reference as おじさん (old man/uncle), a defining character trait where she jokingly refers to herself in masculine terms.

TextENGNew: And with that, she went skipping off happily.
Tch... maybe I went a bit too easy on her. But she's a girl, so... whatever, I guess.
"Hm? ........Wait, WHAT?!"
I happened to glance at my hand. Pitch black. What—WHAT?! The handle was completely smeared with ink! W-When did she even DO that?!
She got me! That little—!
I spun around and there she was: pulling down her eyelid and sticking out her tongue. Classic Mion—even when she's losing, she never loses.
